title: Monthly average surface temperatures by decade
subtitle: The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.
caption:
Monthly average surface temperatures by decade, World

The temperature of the air measured 2 meters above the ground, encompassing land, sea, and in-land water surfaces.

This line chart plots the monthly average surface temperature across the calendar year for eight distinct decades, ranging from the 1940s to the 2020s. The vertical axis measures temperature in degrees Celsius, spanning from 12°C to 17°C, while the horizontal axis marks the months of the year from 1 (January) to 12 (December). The data displays a consistent seasonal cycle for every decade, with temperatures rising from the start of the year to a peak in the middle months before declining toward the end. The lines are vertically stacked, showing a clear progression where more recent decades occupy higher positions on the chart than earlier ones. The 2020s line sits at the top, reaching a maximum value just under 17°C around month 7, while the 1940s line forms the bottom boundary, peaking slightly above 15.5°C in the same period. Intermediate decades follow this order, with the 2010s, 2000s, and 1990s clustered in the upper half of the range, and the 1980s, 1970s, 1960s, and 1950s grouped in the lower half. The vertical gap between the highest and lowest lines remains visible across all months, indicating a consistent temperature difference between the earliest and latest recorded periods. The decadal average for 2020 comprises fewer than the typical 10 data points, which may introduce greater uncertainty compared to other decades. The lines show a systematic upward shift in average monthly temperatures as the decades progress from the 1940s to the 2020s.
